scenario: Scenario 11 -- Human valve and policy-edit commands map onto the orchestrator surface
reason: Top-of-pyramid acceptance/integration test tier under crates/console-cli/tests/ (the Scenario 7 precedent). All three of the scenario's scenes are now built and bound, each riding the shared orchestrator-action port and work_item outcome family: the Approve scene lands crates/console-cli/tests/scenario_11_approve_valve.rs (con-S1), the Reject scene lands crates/console-cli/tests/scenario_11_reject_valve.rs (con-S2), and the set-admission policy-edit scene lands crates/console-cli/tests/scenario_11_set_admission.rs (con-S3). The bound test path names the set-admission scene that completes coverage; the scenario is fully covered. The v035 dispatcher-settings re-baseline reworded two of this scenario's clause lines (the `drive` 1:1 mapping sentence and the TUI drive-the-valves clause), so their gap-ids were rebound; the TUI clause now names six commands and binds to Scenario 10, whose override scene is still pending a test.

scenario: Scenario 13 -- Operator distinguishes cockpit-blind from factory-idle
reason: Top-of-pyramid render-level test asserting the idle-vs-blind header distinction: unavailable sources are counted and named in the header, and a healthy cycle shows no phantom unavailability count. Extended with four source-availability-honesty assertions (b1-source-availability-honesty): a reachable source is counted available and shows no unavailability indicator; a reached-but-empty source (an empty work-item ledger, zero open pull requests, or an absent-but-expected dispatch journal) is observed-and-idle, never counted or named unavailable; a recovered source clears from the tally on its next successful observation (latest-poll-outcome-per-source, not any historical failure); and a genuinely-unreachable source is counted and named with a durably-persisted human-readable reason. Binds the new Adapter Contract honesty clause (empty-is-not-unavailable / persisted-reason / latest-per-source).

scenario: Scenario 18 -- Operator opens pane-specific modal help and exits only with Esc
reason: Pending top-of-pyramid render + loop-level test for the pane-specific modal Help overlay (impl commitment tui-pane-specific-modal-help-overlay): `?` opens the overlay auto-focused to the focused pane's section, with a Global-actions section plus one per focusable pane; the overlay is a 3-character-border near-full-viewport window that never renders wider than the viewport; a left-side section menu beside a right-side help-text pane that scrolls up and down only; and a modal that always prints `esc to exit` and closes only on Esc, with any other key leaving the underlying view unswitched and unscrolled. Binds the new TUI Contract modal-help clause.

scenario: Scenario 9 -- Operator sets a dispatcher policy setting from the console
reason: Top-of-pyramid acceptance test for the dispatcher-settings write path, driving the console command pump end to end over the real DispatcherOrchestratorActionPort: the single-setting `config.dispatcher_setting_set` command, the ordinary recorded write with no arming ceremony, the orchestrator-owned setting state the console never duplicates (the write rides `drive.py --action set-config:<key>:<value>`, never a `.livespec.jsonc` write), the `config.dispatcher_setting.changed` audit with previous/new values, and the not-wired honesty outcome. The Settings-surface TUI slice (six-row content pane, per-setting detail help, and the Enter/Space edit that emits the command with no confirm modal) is unit-covered in console-application and console-tui; this acceptance test covers the handler round-trip.

scenario: Scenario 10 -- A per-item override beats the global default, except `wip_cap`
reason: Pending top-of-pyramid acceptance test for the per-item override valve: an override beating the global default, the `wip_cap` rejection (a per-repo concurrency ceiling admits none), the one-console-command-per-overridable-setting rule, and the TUI driving all six Work-item Lifecycle commands. The six-command TUI clause binds here rather than to Scenario 11 because Scenario 11 is fully covered by built tests while the sixth (override) scene is not yet built. Tier: top-of-pyramid acceptance, under crates/console-cli/tests/. Owed by the W3 Settings-surface code child, which lands the per-item override control.

scenario: Scenario 14 -- Settings surface stays in lockstep with the orchestrator's declared keys
reason: Top-of-pyramid coverage for the consumer-side API-to-Settings-to-help-to-doc completeness check (console-completeness-check): it reads the orchestrator's PUBLISHED declared-key surface (the captured config-manifest, not a hardcoded list) and fails naming any declared key missing from the Settings rows, its inline help, or the README settings doc. Sibling cases evaluate_names_a_key_whose_row_has_no_inline_help, evaluate_names_a_key_missing_from_the_readme_settings_doc, and console_settings_rows_surface_every_dispatcher_row_with_help complete the pyramid.

scenario: Scenario 16 -- Factory drain passes the Dispatcher no policy-arming argument
reason: Pending top-of-pyramid acceptance test for the factory-drain launcher argv: the drain port invokes the Dispatcher with NO per-run policy-arming argument, leaving dispatch-time policy to the orchestrator-owned `dispatcher.*` settings the Dispatcher reads itself. A per-run flag writes no setting, so this is a distinct obligation from the settings-write scenarios and earns its own bound test. Tier: top-of-pyramid acceptance, under crates/console-cli/tests/. Owed by the drain-launcher code child; W1 already fixed the shipped launcher, so this test pins that fix against regression rather than driving new behavior.

scenario: Scenario 17 -- Operator selects a work-item and moves it along the pipeline
reason: Top-of-pyramid proof of the operator status-move surface: selecting a lane work-item and confirming the move-to-status valve persists the command the shared orchestrator port dispatches. It anchors the two re-derived command-count clauses (the eight-command / seven-map-1:1 Command-Handling and TUI-Contract MUST lines), which now assert the move and resolve-blocked commands issue through the drive surface; the sibling tests move_handler_maps_each_pre_terminal_target_onto_the_move_action_id, move_handler_rejects_ship_guarded_and_malformed_targets_and_empty_ids, and valve_and_move_status_keys_fire_on_a_selected_lane_item complete the pyramid across the broad move, the ship-guard, and the picker firing on a selected lane item.
